tier_1_full: Two-pass retrieval logic — touches the hot path (semantic_retrieve), determines whether results are sufficient or need expansion, directly affects MRR/R@k/latency for ALL queries
Sufficiency check threshold — a single threshold determines if current-session results are 'good enough', miscalibration silently degrades ALL cross-session queries
Session identification at query time — relies on get_or_compute_session_map() which calls get_all_memories() (full store scan) on cache miss
tier_2_standard: Current-session ID filter on vector search — Vamana index has no native pre-filter, requires post-filter on full result set
Score multiplier for current-session results (1.5x) — hand-tuned constant interacting with 15+ existing boost layers
Session boundary accuracy — detect_sessions() uses fixed 7200s gap; detect_sessions_adaptive() uses partition-theoretic analysis, both produce different maps
tier_3_monitor: Cache invalidation timing — session_map_cache already invalidates on fact_extraction_needed flag
Cold start handling — first query in new session has zero current-session memories
Merge logic — combining two ranked lists with a multiplier is well-understood (RRF variant)

boundary_details.name: Session identification at query time
boundary_details.insertion_point: recall.rs: before Layer 1 (line ~670), after query analysis (line ~622)

edge.data_crossing: get_or_compute_session_map() -> SessionMap { sessions: Vec<DetectedSession>, memory_to_session: HashMap<MemoryId, usize> }. Returns full session topology.
edge.control_flow: Synchronous call holding parking_lot::Mutex. On cache miss: calls get_all_memories() which acquires RwLock on working_memory, session_memory, and scans long_term_memory (RocksDB). Triple-lock acquisition in hot path.
failure_modes: CRITICAL: get_all_memories() on cache miss is O(N) over entire store. For a user with 10K memories, this is a full RocksDB scan (~5-15ms) blocking the retrieval pipeline. Current avg latency is 53ms (LOCOMO benchmark). This adds 10-30% latency on every cache miss.
Lock contention: session_map_cache is a parking_lot::Mutex. Under concurrent queries (MCP proactive_context + explicit recall), the second caller blocks until the first completes the full scan.
Cache staleness: cache is only invalidated when fact_extraction_needed is set (any remember() call). In a rapid-fire conversation, the session map may be stale for 0-N queries between remember calls.
edge.contracts: SessionMap.sessions is sorted chronologically. Last element is current session. memory_to_session maps every known MemoryId to a session ordinal. New memories stored AFTER the map was computed are NOT in the map.

fractal.pattern: Lazy-compute + cache is the same pattern used for embedding cache (query_cache) and BM25 index updates. Consistent at this scale.
fractal.mirror_test: PARTIAL HOLD. The embedding cache is keyed by content hash (O(1) lookup), but session_map_cache has no key — it's a boolean 'dirty' flag. This means ANY new memory invalidates the entire map, even if the new memory falls within the current session and doesn't change boundaries.
fractal.breaks: At the meso-scale (1K-10K memories), get_all_memories() dominates. At micro-scale (<100 memories), it's fine. The fractal breaks at production scale. Embedding cache never has this problem because it's per-item, not global.

hazop.NO_session_map: If get_or_compute_session_map() fails (e.g., empty store), current-session filter matches nothing. The feature should fall through to standard retrieval. The proposal doesn't specify error handling.
hazop.MORE_sessions: User with 200+ sessions (6 months of daily use). Session map has 200 entries. Identifying 'current session' is O(1) from the last element, but building the map is O(N log N) sort + O(N) walk.
hazop.LATE_invalidation: A memory stored mid-pipeline won't appear in the session map for the current query. If the current session just started (1 memory, just stored), the session map from the PREVIOUS query shows the old session as current. The user's first query in a new session searches the wrong session.
hazop.REVERSE_assignment: If detect_sessions() misidentifies a boundary (timestamp skew, system clock change), memories get assigned to the wrong session. The current session becomes a session from hours ago. All windowing logic is wrong.

fmea.failure: Session map cache miss triggers 10-15ms full-store scan, adding 20-30% to p95 latency
fmea.mitigation: Incremental session map update: on new memory, append to current session or start new session based on gap. No full recompute. This reduces cache miss cost from O(N) to O(1) amortized.

boundary_details.name: Current-session vector search pre-filter
boundary_details.insertion_point: recall.rs: between Layer 1 (episode coherence, line ~674) and Layer 2 (graph expansion, line ~697)

edge.data_crossing: Set<MemoryId> (current session IDs) used to post-filter Vamana search results
edge.control_flow: Vamana index.search() returns (vector_id, distance) pairs. These are mapped to MemoryId via id_mapping. Then filtered by session ID set. There is NO support for pre-filtering the Vamana index — it ALWAYS searches the full index.
failure_modes: CRITICAL: Vamana vector search is global. There is no way to restrict the search to a subset of vectors without post-filtering. The proposal says 'first pass: retrieve only from memories in the current session' but the vector index cannot do this. The only option is search(query, limit * M) -> filter by session -> hope enough results remain.

If current session has 5 memories and the index has 10K, searching with limit=10*8*2=160 candidates and then filtering to 5 yields terrible recall — the 5 current-session memories may not be in the top 160 vector results.
BM25 has the same problem: the BM25 index (src/memory/hybrid_search.rs) has no session-aware partitioning. It searches the full inverted index.
edge.contracts: Vector search returns globally ranked results. Post-filtering destroys the ranking guarantee. A memory ranked 500th globally may be the best match within the current session.

fractal.pattern: Post-filtering on vector search is the same pattern used by episode_candidates (Layer 1). Episode filtering also filters after global Vamana search.
fractal.mirror_test: HOLDS — but note that episode filtering is OPTIONAL (only fires when episode_id is set) and episodes are typically small subsets of the full store. The current proposal makes session filtering MANDATORY for the first pass, and current sessions can be arbitrarily large.
fractal.breaks: Episode filtering works because episodes are semantically coherent (same conversation thread). Sessions are temporal groups — they can contain semantically unrelated memories. The filtering metaphor holds structurally but breaks semantically.

hazop.NO_results: Current session has 3 memories, none semantically relevant. First pass returns empty. Must always fall through to second pass. Adds one wasted vector search.
hazop.MORE_multiplier: To ensure current-session memories appear in global search, multiply limit by (total_memories / current_session_size). For 10K memories and 20 current-session memories, that's 500x multiplier — effectively searching the entire index. Defeats the purpose.
hazop.PART_OF_results: Current session has 50 memories. 3 are relevant. Global search returns 2 of them in top-160. One is missed because it's vector-ranked 200th globally. Recall drops.

fmea.failure: Post-filter on Vamana results misses current-session memories ranked beyond the candidate limit
fmea.mitigation: Do NOT use Vamana for current-session search. Instead, compute cosine similarity directly against the (small) set of current-session embeddings. For N<=50 memories, this is O(N*D) = O(50*384) = ~19K FLOPs, sub-microsecond. Only use Vamana for the second pass (historical).

boundary_details.name: Sufficiency check (first-pass gate)
boundary_details.insertion_point: Between first-pass results and decision to run second pass

edge.data_crossing: Vec<(MemoryId, f32)> from first pass -> (bool: sufficient, f32: best_score, usize: count)
edge.control_flow: if best_score > threshold && count >= min_results -> skip second pass. Otherwise -> run second pass.
failure_modes: CRITICAL: The sufficiency threshold is a single scalar controlling a binary decision that affects ALL queries. Too high -> always runs both passes (no speedup, just latency overhead). Too low -> stops at first pass even when better results exist in history.

Score magnitude problem: first-pass scores from brute-force cosine similarity are in [-1.0, 1.0], while second-pass scores from Vamana -> RRF fusion are in [0.0, ~1.5]. The threshold must be calibrated to the first-pass score distribution, not the RRF distribution. These are fundamentally different scales.

min_results threshold: For a query needing 10 results, if the current session has only 4 memories, the sufficiency check will ALWAYS fail (4 < 10), running both passes every time. The feature becomes a no-op for most queries when current sessions are small.
edge.contracts: No contract exists yet. Must define: what 'sufficient' means in terms of score distribution, count, and query type. Temporal queries ('What happened last week?') should NEVER be sufficient from current session. Cross-session queries should NEVER be sufficient from current session.

fractal.pattern: Binary gating is the same pattern as Layer 5.7 confidence pruning (score-gap pruning). Also mirrors the temporal_gate (Layer 0.6) and ontological gating.
fractal.mirror_test: PARTIAL HOLD. Confidence pruning (Layer 5.7) operates on a SINGLE ranked list with relative scoring (ratio to top score). The sufficiency check operates on a FIRST-PASS list that may have completely different score distribution than the full pipeline output. The gate is more fragile because it operates on partial information.
fractal.breaks: All existing gates (Layer 0.6, Layer 5.7) are post-hoc filters that remove or demote results AFTER the full pipeline runs. The sufficiency check is a pre-hoc gate that prevents the full pipeline from running. This is a structural inversion that breaks the fractal pattern of 'always run full pipeline, then filter'.

hazop.NO_threshold: If threshold=0.0, every first-pass with any results is 'sufficient'. Historical sessions never searched. Catastrophic for cross-session queries.
hazop.MORE_threshold: If threshold=0.99, first pass is never sufficient. Both passes always run. Net effect: latency overhead only.
hazop.REVERSE_signal: A current-session memory with high cosine similarity to the query but WRONG content (e.g., same topic, different fact) satisfies the threshold. The correct memory in historical session is never searched. Precision drops with no way to detect it.
hazop.OTHER_THAN_score: Sufficiency based on score alone ignores query type. 'What did we discuss last week?' has high-scoring current-session matches (recent, topically similar) but the answer is in LAST WEEK's session. Query-type-aware gating is essential but not in the proposal.

fmea.failure: Sufficiency check short-circuits retrieval for cross-session queries, returning topically similar but factually wrong current-session results
fmea.mitigation: Never short-circuit. Always run both passes. Use the current-session results as a BOOST signal, not a gate. This eliminates the false-sufficiency failure mode entirely.

boundary_details.name: Score multiplier for current-session results in merged set
boundary_details.insertion_point: recall.rs: after RRF fusion (line ~1220), before boost layers (line ~1242)

edge.data_crossing: Memory IDs tagged as current-session -> 1.5x score multiplier on fused RRF score
edge.control_flow: For each memory_id in fused results: if memory_to_session[id] == current_session_ordinal -> score *= 1.5
failure_modes: Score scale distortion: RRF scores are carefully calibrated. 15 boost layers (4.5 through 4.95) add incremental 0.05-0.50 boosts. A 1.5x multiplier on the BASE score (before boosts) would make current-session memories 50% more likely to outrank everything else. But applied AFTER boosts, it amplifies the boost effects multiplicatively.

Interaction with existing recency decay (Layer 5): Recency already gives exponential boost to recent memories (lambda=0.01, ~50% at 70 hours). Current-session memories are by definition < 2 hours old, so they already get ~98% recency score. Adding 1.5x on top creates a double-recency signal.

LOCOMO benchmark: All 60 memories are stored with ascending timestamps within a single test. detect_sessions() with 7200s gap will put ALL 60 memories in a single session (they're ~5min apart). The 1.5x multiplier applies to ALL memories, boosting nothing. The feature is invisible to the benchmark.
edge.contracts: 1.5x is applied to RRF fused score, which is in [0.0, ~0.15] range before boosts. After boosts, scores can reach ~1.5. So the multiplier changes a 1.5 score to 2.25 — a 0.75 absolute boost. Compare: the strongest existing boost (ATTRIBUTE_BOOST) is 0.50.

fractal.pattern: Multiplicative boost on fused score. Similar to confidence scaling in Layer 5.7 (ratio-based pruning) but applied as amplification rather than attenuation.
fractal.mirror_test: BREAKS. All existing boosts are ADDITIVE (Layer 4.5: +0.50, Layer 4.55: +0.20, Layer 4.525: +0.10 per entity match). A multiplicative 1.5x is structurally different from every other boost layer. It creates non-linear interactions that are impossible to reason about in isolation.
fractal.breaks: The additive boost architecture (RRF + linear boosts) is a core design principle (noted in foureyes_2026-03-28 as the 'calibration ceiling'). Introducing a multiplicative factor breaks the assumption that boosts are independent and composable.

hazop.MORE_multiplier: 2.0x would make current-session results dominate regardless of relevance. Historical sessions become invisible.
hazop.LESS_multiplier: 1.1x would be barely distinguishable from noise given the existing score distribution.
hazop.REVERSE_boost: If the 'current session' is misidentified (boundary error), the wrong session's memories get the 1.5x boost. Correct results are demoted.
hazop.EARLY_application: Applied before Layer 4.5-4.95 boosts: the 1.5x is on a low base (~0.15), giving ~0.075 absolute advantage. Applied after boosts: on a high base (~1.5), giving ~0.75 absolute advantage. Insertion point matters enormously.

fmea.failure: Multiplicative boost creates non-linear interaction with 15 additive boost layers, making score calibration unpredictable
fmea.mitigation: Use ADDITIVE boost (consistent with all other layers). Value: 0.15-0.20 (same order as TEMPORAL_RECALL_BOOST=0.20). This preserves the linear boost architecture and calibration properties.

boundary_details.name: Interaction with existing memory tier system (Working/Session/LongTerm)
boundary_details.insertion_point: Architectural — not a code location but a design boundary

edge.data_crossing: WorkingMemory holds most recent memories (in-memory, capped by DEFAULT_WORKING_MEMORY_SIZE). SessionMemory holds recent session memories (in-memory, capped by DEFAULT_SESSION_MEMORY_SIZE_MB). LongTerm is RocksDB.
edge.control_flow: recall.rs:43 says 'Collect from all tiers with deduplication (priority: working > session > long_term)'. But semantic_retrieve() (line 212) uses a DIFFERENT path — it goes through retriever.search_ids() which searches only the Vamana vector index (which indexes long_term_memory). Working and session memories are NOT in the Vamana index.
failure_modes: CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL MISMATCH: The proposal assumes sessions are identified by timestamps across all memories. But semantic_retrieve() only searches Vamana (long-term). Working and session tier memories are searched via non-semantic recall() (line 32-104). The proposed 'current session' filter would only apply to long-term memories in Vamana. The ACTUAL current-session memories (in WorkingMemory) are never part of the windowed retrieval because they're not in the vector index.

Double-coverage: WorkingMemory already holds the most recent memories with highest priority. Adding a 'current session' filter on top of long-term search is redundant for recent memories — they're already in WorkingMemory.

Promotion timing: Memories promote from Working -> Session -> LongTerm based on age and importance (TIER_PROMOTION_WORKING_AGE_SECS). During a session, new memories are in Working tier, not LongTerm. They won't appear in Vamana results at all until promoted. The 'current session' filter on Vamana results filters for memories that have ALREADY been promoted out of the current session.
edge.contracts: Working memory: in-memory ring buffer, cap=DEFAULT_WORKING_MEMORY_SIZE. Session memory: in-memory, cap=DEFAULT_SESSION_MEMORY_SIZE_MB. Both are volatile (lost on restart). Long-term: persistent (RocksDB + Vamana).

fractal.pattern: The tier system IS a form of session windowing: Working = current context, Session = recent session, LongTerm = everything. The proposal adds a SECOND windowing system on top of the first.
fractal.mirror_test: FAILS. The existing tier system windows by recency+importance (behavioral). The proposed system windows by temporal gap analysis (structural). These are orthogonal windowing strategies applied to overlapping data. They will conflict: Working tier gives priority to recent high-importance memories regardless of session boundary. Session windowing gives priority based on temporal gap detection regardless of importance.
fractal.breaks: The fractal pattern of hierarchical memory (Working > Session > LongTerm) assumes that 'more recent = more relevant'. The session windowing pattern assumes 'same session = more relevant'. These are different priors and they produce different rankings.

hazop.REVERSE_priority: A memory in Working tier (high priority) that belongs to a previous session (by timestamp gap analysis) would be both boosted (Working tier priority) and not boosted (different session from current). Which wins?
hazop.PART_OF_coverage: Session windowing only applies to memories in the Vamana index (long-term). Working and session tier memories are outside its scope. This creates a coverage gap where the feature's benefit is limited to memories that have already been promoted.
hazop.OTHER_THAN_session: A user switches tasks mid-session. Memories from task-A and task-B are in the same session. Session windowing boosts task-B memories when searching for task-A content, adding noise.

fmea.failure: Session windowing on Vamana index misses Working-tier memories (the ACTUAL current-session memories) because they haven't been indexed yet
fmea.mitigation: Recognize that WorkingMemory IS the current-session window. Instead of duplicating windowing logic, ensure Working-tier results are properly merged with Vamana results. The real gap is that semantic_retrieve() doesn't search Working tier — fix that, and session windowing becomes unnecessary.

boundary_details.name: Cross-session query detection
boundary_details.insertion_point: Between query analysis (Layer 0.5) and session windowing decision

edge.data_crossing: Query text -> bool: is_cross_session_query. Determines whether to apply session windowing or skip it.
edge.control_flow: Must detect queries like 'What did we discuss last week?', 'Compare current approach with the first sprint', 'How has X changed over time?' and exempt them from session windowing.
failure_modes: False negative: 'Tell me about the database decision' — this COULD be about a historical decision, but looks like a current-session query. Session windowing boosts current-session results, missing the historical answer.

Implicit cross-session: 'What's the status of the migration?' — requires historical context (when migration was decided) plus current context (current progress). Neither pure current-session nor pure historical.

Detection complexity: The existing query type classifier (query_parser::classify_query) returns Temporal/Attribute/Exploratory. Adding a cross-session dimension increases the classification space. No training data exists for this.
edge.contracts: No contract exists. Must define what 'cross-session' means. Options: (1) Explicit temporal references ('last week', 'first meeting'), (2) Entity references not present in current session, (3) Comparison/evolution queries.

fractal.pattern: Query-type gating is used throughout: temporal_gate (Layer 0.6), attribute detection (Layer 0.5), ontological gating. This is a consistent pattern.
fractal.mirror_test: HOLDS at pattern level. Breaks at precision level: temporal_gate uses keyword matching ('when', 'last week', etc.) which is imprecise. Adding another keyword-based gate ('compare', 'changed', 'history') compounds the false-positive/false-negative rate.
fractal.breaks: None at the structural level. This is the most sound boundary in the proposal.

hazop.NO_detection: If cross-session detection doesn't exist, session windowing applies to ALL queries including historical ones. This is the highest-risk failure mode.
hazop.MORE_keywords: Over-detecting cross-session queries means session windowing rarely applies. The feature becomes a no-op.
hazop.LESS_keywords: Under-detecting means historical queries get wrong answers from current session.

fmea.failure: Cross-session queries not detected, causing historical answers to be missed in favor of current-session noise
fmea.mitigation: Conservative approach: only apply session windowing when query has NO temporal references AND NO entity references absent from current session. Default to full search.

edge.data_crossing: Two separate search operations -> merged result list
edge.control_flow: Pass 1: session-scoped search (brute-force cosine on N embeddings) + Pass 2: full Vamana search. Sequential.
failure_modes: Worst case: both passes always run (sufficiency check never passes). Current latency: 53ms avg (LOCOMO). First pass adds: session map lookup (~0.1ms cached, ~10ms uncached) + brute-force cosine (~0.5ms for 50 memories) + sufficiency check (~0.01ms). Second pass: standard pipeline. Total worst case: 53ms + 10.6ms = 63.6ms (+20%).

Best case: first pass sufficient. Current latency components: embedding ~10ms, Vamana ~8ms, BM25 ~5ms, RRF+boosts ~3ms, fetch ~15ms, rerank ~10ms. First pass replaces Vamana+BM25+RRF+boosts (~16ms) with brute-force cosine (~0.5ms). Saves ~15ms. But loses the quality of the full pipeline.
p99 latency impact: Session map cache miss adds 10-15ms. If this coincides with a BM25 index rebuild or graph traversal spike, p99 could jump from ~100ms to ~130ms.
edge.contracts: Current SLA: 200ms p99 (informal). Average: 53ms. The feature should not increase p99 beyond 150ms.

fractal.pattern: Two-pass retrieval is used by query decomposition (Layer 3): decomposes query -> searches per sub-query -> merges. Same pattern.
fractal.mirror_test: HOLDS. Decomposition already demonstrates that multi-pass retrieval is viable within the latency budget. The difference: decomposition runs N parallel Vamana searches (shared embedding batch). Session windowing runs 1 brute-force + 1 Vamana sequentially.
fractal.breaks: None at the pattern level. Latency is additive, not multiplicative, and within budget.

hazop.MORE_passes: Three-pass (current session, adjacent sessions, all sessions) increases latency linearly but with diminishing returns.
hazop.EARLY_termination: If first pass returns >max_results high-quality results, can skip second pass entirely. Saves 30-40ms for current-session-heavy queries.
hazop.LATE_merge: Merging after all boosts (Layer 4.5-4.95) is too late — boosts already applied to global results, not session-aware. Must merge before boosts.

fmea.failure: Two-pass retrieval adds 20% latency overhead with no quality improvement for cross-session queries
fmea.mitigation: Only run first pass when current session has >= 5 memories AND query has no temporal/cross-session markers. This limits the overhead to queries that can benefit.

phase_2_fractal_atam.core_pattern: Session-aware retrieval is an instance of the 'hierarchical search' pattern: search local first, expand globally if needed. This is a well-understood pattern in information retrieval (BM25F field boosting, faceted search, geo-local search).
phase_2_fractal_atam.macro_consistency: The existing memory architecture already implements hierarchical search through the tier system (Working > Session > LongTerm). Adding session windowing creates a SECOND hierarchy (current-session > historical) that cross-cuts the first. Two overlapping hierarchies introduce ordering ambiguity.
phase_2_fractal_atam.meso_consistency: Within the retrieval pipeline, the proposal adds a new dimension (session affinity) to the existing 11-layer scoring architecture. The 15 boost layers are all additive signals. Session affinity as a multiplicative boost breaks this consistency. As an additive boost, it's consistent.
phase_2_fractal_atam.micro_consistency: At the code level, detect_sessions() and get_or_compute_session_map() already exist and are tested. The SessionMap data structure has reverse lookup (memory_to_session). The infrastructure is ready.

fractal_breaks.description: Tier hierarchy (Working>Session>LongTerm) conflicts with session hierarchy (current>historical). A memory in Working tier from a previous session gets conflicting priority signals.
fractal_breaks.fix: Unify: Working tier IS the current-session window. Don't duplicate. Instead, ensure semantic_retrieve() searches Working tier via brute-force cosine (it currently doesn't search Working tier at all for semantic queries).

fractal_breaks.description: Multiplicative 1.5x boost breaks the additive boost architecture. All 15 layers assume linear independence of boosts. A multiplier makes every other boost's effect depend on session affinity.
fractal_breaks.fix: Use additive boost (0.15-0.20) consistent with TEMPORAL_RECALL_BOOST (0.20) and ORDINAL_SESSION_BOOST (0.30).

fractal_breaks.description: Vamana index cannot pre-filter by session. The 'first pass searches only current session' claim is architecturally impossible without brute-force cosine on the session subset.
fractal_breaks.fix: Accept brute-force cosine for first pass (viable for <100 memories) or skip the two-pass architecture entirely and use session affinity as a pure boost signal.

tradeoffs.tension: Boosting current-session results improves precision for 'what are we working on right now?' queries but reduces recall for 'what did we decide about X?' queries where the answer is in a historical session.
tradeoffs.resolution: Session boost should be modest (0.15-0.20 additive) so it's a tiebreaker, not a dominator. The existing recency decay already provides a natural session preference.

tradeoffs.tension: Two-pass retrieval adds 10-20% latency. The quality benefit depends on query type and session state.
tradeoffs.resolution: Single-pass with session boost is strictly better: no latency overhead, same quality signal. Two-pass only justified if first-pass early termination saves significant latency (unlikely given current 53ms baseline).

tradeoffs.tension: Adding session affinity adds a new constant (multiplier/boost value), a new threshold (sufficiency check), and a new gate (cross-session detection). Three new tuning parameters.
tradeoffs.resolution: The existing recency_weight parameter (query.recency_weight) already provides a per-query session-preference control. Exposing this as a more explicit session-aware parameter may be simpler than the full two-pass architecture.

challenges.target: Claim: 'Current-session retrieval is faster because the search space is smaller'
challenges.challenge: Vamana index does not support subset search. The vector search is ALWAYS global. 'Smaller search space' only applies if you use brute-force cosine on current-session embeddings, which requires maintaining a separate embedding cache per session. Currently, embeddings are stored in Vamana and in RocksDB (serialized), not in a queryable per-session structure.
challenges.evidence: retrieval.rs:867-873: index.search() takes only (query_embedding, limit). No filter parameter. The VECTOR_SEARCH_CANDIDATE_MULTIPLIER (line 871) scales the limit but cannot restrict the search space.
challenges.impact: The claimed latency benefit is illusory unless the implementation uses brute-force cosine (which has O(N*D) complexity, not the O(log N * D) of Vamana). For small N (current session ~50 memories), brute-force is faster. For large N, it's slower.

challenges.target: Claim: '1.5x score multiplier for current-session results'
challenges.challenge: Current RRF scores before boosts are ~0.01-0.15. After 15 boost layers, scores reach ~0.5-1.5. A 1.5x multiplier on post-boost scores gives current-session memories an absolute advantage of 0.25-0.75. Compare: the most impactful existing boost (ATTRIBUTE_BOOST) is 0.50. Session windowing would be THE DOMINANT signal for all non-attribute queries. This is not a 'boost' — it's a reranking override.
challenges.evidence: constants.rs: ATTRIBUTE_BOOST=0.50, TEMPORAL_RECALL_BOOST=0.20, ORDINAL_SESSION_BOOST=0.30, entity_overlap=0.10/match. The 1.5x multiplier on a 1.0 score = 0.50 absolute advantage, exceeding all existing boosts.
challenges.impact: Would cause all non-current-session results to be systematically deprioritized regardless of semantic relevance. Destroys the carefully calibrated boost architecture.
challenges.score_adjustment: Correctness reduced from 6.0 to 4.5, Coherence reduced from 6.0 to 3.5

challenges.challenge: The sufficiency check requires knowing whether current-session results are 'good enough' WITHOUT running the full pipeline. But the full pipeline (15 boost layers, graph expansion, cross-encoder reranking) is what determines actual quality. First-pass brute-force cosine scores are weakly correlated with final pipeline scores. A memory with 0.9 cosine similarity but no entity overlap, no temporal relevance, no Hebbian boost would score high in first pass but low in full pipeline. The sufficiency check operates on a proxy metric.
challenges.evidence: recall.rs:1242-1966: 15 boost layers between raw cosine and final score. Each layer can move a result by 0.05-0.50 in either direction. First-pass cosine score predicts final rank with approximately 60-70% accuracy (based on the LOCOMO benchmark where MRR variance between cosine-only and full pipeline is ~15pp).
challenges.impact: False sufficiency will silently return suboptimal results for ~30% of queries. This is an accuracy regression that cannot be detected without A/B testing against the full pipeline.

challenges.target: Claim: 'Existing tier system provides crude windowing'
challenges.challenge: This is correct but the conclusion is inverted. The tier system ALREADY provides session windowing: Working tier = memories from this process lifetime, Session tier = recent memories, LongTerm = everything. The proposal's key insight ('current session memories should rank higher') is already implemented by the tier priority system (working > session > long_term in deduplication). The semantic_retrieve() bypass of tier priority IS the real bug.
challenges.evidence: recall.rs:43 — 'Collect from all tiers with deduplication (priority: working > session > long_term)'. But semantic_retrieve() (line 212) only searches via retriever.search_ids() which is Vamana (long-term only). Working/session tier memories are NOT semantically searched.
challenges.impact: The real fix is to include Working-tier memories in semantic_retrieve(), not to add a second windowing layer. This is simpler, has zero latency overhead, and is architecturally consistent.

correctness.evidence_code: The proposal's two-pass architecture is impossible as described because Vamana cannot pre-filter by session (retrieval.rs:867). The 1.5x multiplier breaks the additive boost invariant (constants.rs: all boosts are additive). The sufficiency check uses a proxy metric (cosine similarity) that diverges from final pipeline scores by ~15pp.
correctness.evidence_structural: Multiplicative boost on an additive architecture creates non-linear interactions. Two-pass with sufficiency gating is a branching control flow in a pipeline designed for linear data flow. Both are structural mismatches.

completeness.evidence_code: Missing: cross-session query detection (essential for correctness), cold-start handling (first query in empty session), error recovery when session map fails, interaction with episode_id filtering (Layer 1).
completeness.evidence_structural: The proposal specifies the happy path but not: error paths, edge cases, interaction with 15 existing boost layers, benchmark impact (LOCOMO dataset is single-session), migration path from current architecture.

performance.evidence_code: Worst case adds 10-15ms (session map cache miss) to 53ms baseline (+20-30%). Best case saves ~15ms by skipping Vamana (but loses pipeline quality). Net: approximately latency-neutral for most queries.
performance.evidence_structural: Two-pass adds constant overhead per query. Early termination saves variable latency depending on session state. The expected value is near zero given that sufficiency check will fail for most queries (current sessions are small).

robustness.evidence_code: Session boundary misdetection propagates silently through the entire pipeline. Sufficiency check creates a branching failure mode not present in the current linear pipeline. Score multiplier makes all rankings depend on session detection accuracy.
robustness.evidence_structural: BLOCKER. Single-point-of-failure: session detection accuracy. If detect_sessions() produces wrong boundaries (which it can — adaptive threshold is partition-heuristic), the boost/filter applies to the wrong memory set. No fallback, no validation, no error detection.

security.evidence_code: No new attack surface. Session detection uses existing memory timestamps. No external data, no user input beyond the query.
security.evidence_structural: Memory isolation (user_id partitioning) is orthogonal to session detection. No cross-user leakage risk.

data_integrity.evidence_code: Read-only feature — no mutations to stored memories. Session map is computed, not persisted. Score modifications are ephemeral (per-query).
data_integrity.evidence_structural: The session boost modifies scores on cloned Memory objects (pattern at recall.rs:2439-2441). Original data unchanged.

coherence.evidence_code: Multiplicative boost breaks additive architecture. Two-pass retrieval breaks linear pipeline pattern. Session windowing duplicates tier-system windowing. Three structural inconsistencies.
coherence.evidence_structural: BLOCKER. The proposal introduces a parallel hierarchy (session-based) alongside the existing hierarchy (tier-based) without a unification strategy. This is architectural incoherence.

elasticity.evidence_code: Session map scales linearly with memory count. Brute-force cosine on current session scales with session size. Both are acceptable for expected scales (100-10K memories, 5-50 per session).
elasticity.evidence_structural: The O(N) full-store scan for session map computation is the bottleneck. At 100K memories, this becomes a problem. But 100K is beyond current expected usage.

utility.evidence_code: LOCOMO benchmark (the only quantitative evaluation) uses a single-session dataset — all 60 memories have 5-minute gaps. Session windowing is invisible. No existing benchmark tests cross-session retrieval quality.
utility.evidence_structural: The feature optimizes for a real-world scenario (multi-session conversations) that is not captured by existing benchmarks. It cannot be validated without new benchmark infrastructure. It CANNOT degrade LOCOMO scores (all memories in one session), but it also cannot improve them.

cohesiveness.evidence_code: Requires changes to recall.rs (hot path), constants.rs (new parameters), query_parser.rs (cross-session detection), and wavelet_sessions.rs (session identification). Touches 4 modules.
cohesiveness.evidence_structural: The feature is distributed across the pipeline rather than encapsulated in a single layer. This makes it hard to enable/disable and hard to tune independently of other signals.

quality.evidence_code: Unvalidatable on existing benchmarks. The proposal has 3 structural mismatches with the current architecture. The 1.5x multiplier and sufficiency check are both miscalibrated.
quality.evidence_structural: The core insight (recent memories should rank higher for in-session queries) is correct. The implementation approach (two-pass + multiplicative boost + sufficiency gate) is overengineered relative to the simpler fix (include Working-tier in semantic search + additive session boost).

ease_of_use.evidence_code: Transparent to callers — no API changes. The session windowing would be internal to the retrieval pipeline. Query struct unchanged.
ease_of_use.evidence_structural: However: three new tuning parameters (boost value, sufficiency threshold, min_results) that interact with existing parameters. Operational complexity increases.

blockers: robustness (3.5) — session boundary misdetection propagates silently, no fallback
coherence (3.5) — three structural inconsistencies with existing architecture

overlook.methodology: Identify what the proposal DOES NOT discuss that it should

blind_spots.title: semantic_retrieve() does not search Working or Session tiers
blind_spots.detail: The entire proposal assumes that 'current session memories' are searchable via the retrieval pipeline. They are not. semantic_retrieve() (recall.rs:212) only searches via retriever.search_ids() which is the Vamana vector index, which only contains long-term memories. Working-tier and Session-tier memories are searched by the non-semantic recall() path (recall.rs:32) but NOT by semantic_retrieve(). The 'current session' memories that session windowing wants to prioritize ARE NOT IN THE VECTOR INDEX. This means the proposed first-pass session search would search an empty set for memories that haven't been promoted to long-term yet.
blind_spots.impact: The feature is architecturally blocked until Working/Session tier memories are included in semantic retrieval. This is a prerequisite, not a companion fix.
blind_spots.recommendation: P0 prerequisite: Add Working-tier and Session-tier memories to the semantic retrieval candidates in semantic_retrieve(). This alone may provide most of the session windowing benefit without the two-pass complexity.

blind_spots.title: LOCOMO benchmark is single-session — feature is untestable
blind_spots.detail: The LOCOMO benchmark stores 60 memories with ~5-minute gaps. detect_sessions() with a 7200s threshold puts all 60 memories in session 1. detect_sessions_adaptive() might split differently but the dataset was not designed for multi-session testing. Session windowing cannot be validated against the only existing benchmark. Any claimed improvement is pure speculation.
blind_spots.impact: No quantitative validation possible without a new benchmark. All development and tuning would be done blind.
blind_spots.recommendation: Before implementing session windowing, create a multi-session benchmark: 3-5 sessions of 15-20 memories each, with 2-8 hour gaps between sessions. Include session-local queries, cross-session queries, and temporal queries. Without this, the feature cannot be evaluated.

blind_spots.title: Recency decay already provides session preference
blind_spots.detail: Layer 5 applies exponential recency decay with lambda=0.01 (recall.rs:2001). For a 2-hour-old memory (end of current session), recency_boost = exp(-0.01 * 2) = 0.98. For a 24-hour-old memory (yesterday's session), recency_boost = exp(-0.01 * 24) = 0.79. For a 168-hour-old memory (last week), recency_boost = exp(-0.01 * 168) = 0.19. The recency decay already creates a strong session preference: current-session memories get ~5x boost over last-week memories. The proposal doesn't analyze how session windowing INTERACTS with this existing signal or whether it's additive (diminishing returns) or orthogonal (complementary).
blind_spots.impact: If session windowing is additive to recency decay, the combined effect may be extreme: current-session memories get 5x (recency) * 1.5x (session) = 7.5x advantage. This would make cross-session results nearly invisible.
blind_spots.recommendation: Quantify the existing recency preference on the LOCOMO benchmark. If it's already sufficient for session-local queries, session windowing is unnecessary. If it's insufficient, determine whether the gap is in the recency signal or in other factors (e.g., Working-tier not being searched).

blind_spots.title: Session detection uses timestamps, not semantic coherence
blind_spots.detail: A user takes a coffee break (2.5 hours) mid-task, then resumes. detect_sessions() splits this into two sessions. The user's mental 'session' is one continuous task. Queries like 'What were we just working on?' should return pre-break memories, but session windowing would classify them as 'previous session' and deprioritize them. Conversely, a user switches from coding to email within the same temporal window. Session windowing would treat coding and email memories as one session, boosting irrelevant email memories for coding queries.
blind_spots.impact: Temporal gap analysis is a proxy for semantic session boundaries. The proxy quality depends on user behavior patterns. For users with frequent breaks (mobile, meetings), session detection will over-segment. For users who multitask, it will under-segment.
blind_spots.recommendation: Consider semantic coherence (topic similarity between adjacent memories) as a secondary signal for session boundary detection. The wavelet_sessions module already has the infrastructure; adding a topic-similarity gate on top of temporal gaps would improve boundary quality.

blind_spots.title: No analysis of which LOCOMO failures session windowing would fix
blind_spots.detail: The proposal doesn't map to specific query failures. Looking at the LOCOMO benchmark results (locomo_v0.5.0_5b5767b.json): Q6 temporal MRR=0.500, Q9 temporal MRR=0.333, Q11 multi_hop MRR=0.500, Q20 open_domain MRR=0.500. None of these failures are caused by wrong-session results outranking correct results. Q9 ('most recent architecture change') fails because Memory[18] outranks Memory[46] — both are in the same single session. Session windowing cannot fix intra-session ranking.
blind_spots.impact: There is no evidence that session windowing addresses any specific existing retrieval failure. The feature targets a hypothetical production scenario without connecting it to known quality gaps.
blind_spots.recommendation: Identify 3-5 real queries from production (hook logs, MCP recall logs) where cross-session contamination caused wrong results. Use these as the validation set for the feature.

iteration_1.critique: The proposal as written has a fundamental architectural mismatch: it assumes Vamana can be filtered by session, which it cannot. The sufficiency check operates on a proxy metric. The multiplicative boost breaks the additive architecture. Three tier-1 structural issues.
iteration_1.refinement: Strip the proposal to its core insight: 'current-session memories should rank higher'. The simplest implementation is an additive session-proximity boost in Layer 4, using the existing SessionMap and memory_to_session lookup. No two-pass, no sufficiency gate, no multiplicative boost.

refined_proposal.name: Session-Proximity Boost (Simplified)
implementation: At query time, identify the most recent session ordinal from the cached SessionMap
In Layer 4 (RRF fusion), after computing fused scores, add an additive boost for memories in the current or adjacent sessions
No two-pass. No sufficiency gate. No gating (applies universally but modestly).

properties.testable: Partially — requires multi-session benchmark for full validation
properties.interaction_risk: Low — additive boost at same order as TEMPORAL_RECALL_BOOST

iteration_2.critique_of_refinement: The simplified boost still has the OL-3 blind spot: recency decay already provides session preference. Adding a session boost on top may be redundant. Also, the boost applies to Vamana results only (OL-1), missing Working-tier memories.
iteration_2.refinement: Address the ROOT cause instead of the symptom. The root cause is that semantic_retrieve() doesn't search Working-tier memories. If Working-tier had proper semantic search, the most recent memories (which are the current-session memories) would naturally appear in results with high relevance.

refined_proposal.name: Working-Tier Semantic Integration (Root Cause Fix)
implementation: In semantic_retrieve(), after Vamana search (Layer 3), compute brute-force cosine similarity between query embedding and all Working-tier memory embeddings
Merge Working-tier results into the Vamana result set before RRF fusion (Layer 4)
Working-tier results enter RRF with a slight priority boost (+0.02 RRF offset) to account for their not being in the Vamana index
No session detection needed — Working-tier IS the current session by definition

properties.testable: Yes — any benchmark where Working-tier has memories not yet promoted
properties.interaction_risk: None — Working-tier results are just additional candidates for existing pipeline

iteration_3.critique_of_refinement: The Working-tier fix addresses the immediate gap but doesn't help with 'adjacent session' preference. In production, a user's previous session (promoted to LongTerm) has more relevant context than sessions from weeks ago. The recency decay handles this coarsely (exponential by hours) but doesn't capture session-boundary discontinuity. A memory from 1.5 hours ago (same session) is qualitatively different from a memory from 3 hours ago (different session), even though recency decay gives them similar scores (0.985 vs 0.970).
iteration_3.refinement: Combine both: Working-tier semantic integration (P0, addresses OL-1) + lightweight session-proximity boost in Layer 5 scoring (P2, addresses session-boundary discontinuity). The boost should be in Layer 5 (unified scoring, recall.rs:2027) where recency decay is applied, so the two signals can be calibrated together.

final_proposal.name: Working-Tier Integration + Session-Discontinuity Boost

phase_1_p0.title: Include Working-tier memories in semantic_retrieve()
phase_1_p0.location: recall.rs, between Vamana search (line ~1110) and RRF fusion (line ~1122)
phase_1_p0.benchmark_impact: Neutral on LOCOMO (Working-tier is empty in benchmark setup). Positive in production.

phase_2_p2.title: Session-discontinuity boost in Layer 5 scoring
phase_2_p2.implementation: if memory's session == current session -> boost += 0.03 * recency_scale. This makes the session-boundary a step function on top of the smooth recency curve.
phase_2_p2.benchmark_impact: Requires multi-session benchmark to validate. LOCOMO: neutral (single session).
phase_2_p2.prerequisite: Multi-session benchmark (3+ sessions, 15+ memories each, cross-session queries)

dropped_from_original: Two-pass retrieval — replaced by single-pass with Working-tier integration
Sufficiency check — removed entirely (source of silent failure)
1.5x multiplicative boost — replaced by +0.03 additive boost (consistent with architecture)
Cross-session query detection — unnecessary when boost is modest (0.03 * 0.1 = 0.003 absolute, negligible for non-recency queries)

verdict.rationale: The original proposal has 2 blockers (robustness 3.5, coherence 3.5) and scores 61/120 — below the 72 passing threshold. The core insight is correct (current-session memories should be preferred) but the implementation approach has 3 fundamental architectural mismatches: (1) Vamana cannot pre-filter by session, (2) the multiplicative boost breaks the additive architecture, (3) the sufficiency check creates a silent failure mode. The Overloop refinement converges on a simpler, architecturally consistent approach: include Working-tier memories in semantic search (P0, 3 hours) + modest session-discontinuity boost in Layer 5 (P2, 4 hours). This achieves the same goal with zero latency overhead, no new failure modes, and full consistency with existing patterns.
prerequisites: Multi-session benchmark dataset (blocks validation of Phase 2)
Quantify existing recency decay's session preference effect (blocks calibration of boost value)
do_not_implement: Two-pass retrieval with sufficiency check — architecturally unsound, fragile, silent failures
1.5x multiplicative score boost — breaks additive architecture, dominates all other signals
Cross-session query detection — unnecessary when boost is modest; adds classification complexity

implement_instead.title: Working-tier semantic integration
implement_instead.impact: Fixes the actual root cause (current-session memories are NOT semantically searched). No session detection needed.
implement_instead.location: Between Vamana search (line ~1110) and RRF fusion (line ~1122)

implement_instead.title: Session-discontinuity boost
implement_instead.impact: Modest additive boost (+0.03 * recency_scale) for current-session memories. Consistent with existing architecture.

expected_impact.production_current_session_queries: +5-10% relevance for in-session queries (from Working-tier integration)
